I don't have a strong opinion on curio's behaviour here, but just noting a GIL-related example that I think is analagous to @njsmith's concern: CPython *consistently* releases the GIL around potentially blocking IO operations, so Pythonistas are accustomed to sock.send()/recv() being reasonably good guarantees that a given thread isn't going to starve other threads of access to the GIL. This is done unconditionally, *before* making the potentially blocking OS level call. The naive interpreter level scheduler can still cause latency spikes when CPU bound threads are mixed with IO bound ones, but IO bound threads will always play nice with each other.

However, I think your main real-world defence against the specific problem being raised here isn't anything to do with the typical relative speeds of clients and servers, but everything to do with typical round trip latencies for TCP packet acknowledgements, and that's where having both the hostile client and the server under attack being on the same system is misleading: instead of there being a ping delay between "data sent by server" and "server receives notification that the data was received by the client", those two events are nigh simultaneous. If I'm right, then putting Vaurien between the client and server with a stable 10+ ms delay in each direction may be enough to eliminate the starvation: http://vaurien.readthedocs.io/en/1.8/#using-vaurien-from-the-command-line

A *UDP* echo server wouldn't have that defence, but UDP servers aren't connection oriented either, so "client starvation" isn't really a thing that can happen the way it can with TCP.
(I think the Oauth-with-GitHub option doesn't help, since that'd cause GitHub to send your server users' tokens over HTTP, and their session cookies would continue to be sent over HTTP too.)

>Would like to have some input from @cr1901 if this should be done for all providers when --cygpath is enabled or if it's specific to svn.

TLDR: Revert my changes until I think of a better solution.

Long story: The more I think about it, the more I'm inclined to feel my solution is a hack.

My philosophy on dealing with the mess on Windows is something like this (substitute `X` for `cygwin`, `msys2`, `native`):
* If using `X`, use the `X` `python` and call out to `X` binaries as much as possible. If calling out to an `X` binary is not possible, try to constrain abstraction leaks as much as possible. See [this issue](https://github.com/m-labs/migen/pull/88) for an example.

One big abstraction leak when mixing binaries from various environments in Windows is path handling. In my case, the `msys2` `svn` doesn't really exist; the `svn` that `msys2` provides is a `cygwin`-like binary that only understands POSIX paths. An `msys2` `svn` would understand both POSIX and Windows paths. So to contain the abstraction leak, I think we should only add [platform-specific logic](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35956180/how-to-determine-the-python-is-mingw-or-official-build) to the OpenCores builder only to handle path conversions.

The travis build log is no surprise this time, as you were [running green against module/package `tests`](https://travis-ci.org/MinchinWeb/verdant-barnacle/jobs/87730614#L144) in a directory where you have only [`README.md` and `verdantbarnacle`](https://travis-ci.org/MinchinWeb/verdant-barnacle/jobs/87730614#L141).

Looking at your first build I do think it is a file case related thing (Windows' file systems are notoriously case ignoring, while travis is running on linux): no tests are run and coveralls has only a coverage for your `__init__.py` - not any tests.

I recommend fixing `.travis.yml`, so that it runs `green -rvv` without any other parameters.

> import datetime

> 
> qwe = re.compile(
>     r'''
>     ^
>     (?P<Year>[0-9]{4})
>     (?P<Month>0[1-9]|1[12])
>     (?P<Day>0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])
>     (?P<Hour>[01][0-9]|2[0-3])
>     (?:
>       (?P<Minute>[0-5][0-9])
>       (?P<Second>[0-5][0-9]|60)?
>     )?
>     (?:
>       [.,]
>       (?P<Fraction>[0-9]+)
>     )?  
>     (?:
>       Z
>       |
>       (?:
>         (?P<Offset>[+-])
>         (?P<OffHour>[01][0-9]|2[0-3])
>         (?P<OffMinute>[0-5][0-9])?
>       )
>     )
>     $
>     ''',
>     re.VERBOSE
> )
> 
> 
> def parse(string: str) -> datetime.datetime:
>     match = qwe.fullmatch(string)
>     if match is None:
>         raise ValueError
>     matches = match.groupdict()
> 
>     offset = datetime.timedelta(
>         hours=int(matches['OffHour'] or 0),
>         minutes=int(matches['OffMinute'] or 0)
>     )
> 
>     if matches['Offset'] == '-':
>         offset *= -1
> 
>     # Python does not support leap second in datetime (!)
>     if matches['Second'] == '60':
>         matches['Second'] = '59'
> 
>     # According to RFC, fraction may be applied to an Hour/Minute (!)
>     fraction = float('0.' + (matches['Fraction'] or '0'))
> 
>     if matches['Minute'] is None:
>         fraction *= 60
>         minute = int(fraction)
>         fraction -= minute
>     else:
>         minute = int(matches['Minute'])
> 
>     if matches['Second'] is None:
>         fraction *= 60
>         second = int(fraction)
>         fraction -= second
>     else:
>         second = int(matches['Second'])
> 
>     microseconds = int(fraction * 1000000)
>

>         int(matches['Month']),
>         int(matches['Day']),
>         int(matches['Hour']),
>         minute,
>         second,
>         microseconds,

Primarily performance, but since you're in python YMMV a lot with regards to how much that matters.

With quamash you're only running one event loop, and plugging he python functions into it. with this you're running two event loops essentially. With quamash you get to use Qts event loop for socket handling which is potentially faster. (there's a re-implementation of the base event loop in C that's faster, I assume using Qts file-descriptor/socket handling is similar).

There's also potentially compatibility concerns if some parts of your code do things the Qt way and some do it using builtin python functions. Then again if everything is done the "python way" in your code instead of the "Qt way" then you probably wouldn't have any problems.

I suppose performance would be a stronger motivator if I could like show charts showing what was faster.

Hi all,
as i am dealing with this issue for the MIREX submission again, some further input:
- stripping of leading or trailing known "silent" segments (denoting the absence of any signal) from ground-truth and predictions should be done in a preprocessing step. It seems we agree that this a not in the scope of mir_eval.
- the first and last resulting boundary _should_ be included in the evaluation (not stripped).
There are two reasons:
1) For the example of SALAMI audio, only a part of the audio files are hard-clipped to the extents of the audio file (trivial boundary). Most have some amount of silence at the beginning or end. Detection of the respective first or last boundary is a truly non-trivial task (e.g., fade-in/out), so to my mind these boundaries should be included in the evaluation. Of course, if the temporal tolerance is high enough (e.g., 3 seconds) most of these transitions will practically coincide with file extents, but the choice of tolerance is another issue.
2) The number of boundaries for many pieces is really low. Stripping two of them voluntarily increases statistical variance considerably. For some pieces there might even not be any other annotated boundaries than just these.

It may not be sufficient, but I think it is not faulty. The test covers the following scenario: 

* Network between client and server has considerable latency.
* Server acks each stanza immediately with a gratuitous ACK (covered by "After enabling stream management, the client or server can send ack elements at any time over the stream.")
* Client sends three stanzas
* Server receives two stanzas and sends corresponding ACKs
* Client receives the first ACK
* Connection is interrupted and stream resumption takes place

The test as it stands is covered by XEP-0198. If we have issues where the resumption has a counter equal to the last received counter, that definitely needs additional testing and fixes, however, the test as it stands is valid I think.

Additionally the line for `x.append(add_to_list(i))` is not allowed within an `@synchronized` function. Currently I think no exceptions are raised (which would be a great improvement) but this program will not work the way you expect.

`@synchronized` functions only support assignment of `@concurrent` function results to index-able objects. So a rewrite might look like:

```python
x = [None] * 100000
@synchronized
def loop_to_add():
    for i in xrange(1, 100000):
        x[i] = add_to_list(i)
    return x
```

I'll have to consider supporting other uses of the return value of `@concurrent` functions like this, at the very least the user should be warned that this will not produce the expected behavior.

The only reason `.lower()` is performed is because I believe that if you're using a case-insensitive keygen, you're probably going to be wanting case-insensitive search, because providing a cased argument to a case-insensitive field is otherwise nonsensical. That's the only strange case here.

With respect to `(...keygen=FULL_TEXT, prefix=True)` + `.startswith(col='an a')`, that's definitely not going to work the way you want, for the reasons you state (words are tokenized as part of indexing process, but not querying).

But no, I won't be adding auto-tokenization to `Query._check` because it would almost immediately invalidate a countless amount of code that already exists that *doesn't* expect tokenization, it wouldn't be forward compatible with new-style keygens (which receives the full entity data before generating a key for the index), and because (with using the keygen) it creates a really odd case where: `.filter(col=['foo', 'bar']) != .filter(col='foo bar')` (the short version is because `col=[...]` is an *OR/UNION* query, but using the keygen as part of the automatic processing of the passed-in string data is an *AND/INTERSECTION* query).

in fact, your example is a good reason for why a default style paradigm *should not* be built into pyexcelerate. what should the fourth column look like? should it be autofit or not? it doesn't have data, so whether or not a default style applies to it is ambiguous. when you manually create the data in excel, you're forced to choose. because pyexcelerate aims to replicate excel's functionalities, the same applies here.

Hi @prijutme4ty!

Thanks for contributing. I have a few issues with the changes so far:

### Overall

* The purpose of this pull request was to make the `c.$tbodies` indexing match the internal cache, but the definition was never changed - it's actually defined twice in the code (line [236](https://github.com/prijutme4ty/tablesorter/blob/master/js/jquery.tablesorter.js#L236) & [1115](https://github.com/prijutme4ty/tablesorter/blob/master/js/jquery.tablesorter.js#L1115)).
* If the `c.$tbodies` were redefined, its change would pretty much break the majority of the currently available widgets - I haven't had time to specifically add an info-only tbody to the examples to test this, but I'm pretty sure there will be issues.
* The purpose of the `c.$tbodies` definition was to only target tbodies that are modified by sorting, and tbodies that contain useful information for widgets. If you need all tbodies, you can just as easily use `c.$table.children('tbody')`; maybe this variable should be renamed to avoid confusion?
* Another issue is that info-only tbodies have no limitations on `colspan` and `rowspan` because the information within them are ignored. Currently, this plugin does not properly support sorting cells with these settings and would likely break the formatting of the table if it attempted to sort, filter or obtain information from those rows within the info-only tbody.
* I know that the unit tests can be fickle and sometimes need to be run more than once to get a clean run, but these changes have broken a few tests.

### Widget Specific

* Setting the pager to always use the first tbody will not work if the first tbody is for information only (such as in [this demo](http://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/4mVfu/4208/); see issue #800).
* Including info-only tbodies with the filter will hide info only tbodies if they don't match the filter.
* I'm sure there would be more issues with widgets, but hopefully I got my point across.

Honestly, I don't think it's a bad idea to make the indexing match the internal cache; but I would prefer to save big changes like this for [Abelt](https://github.com/tablesort/Abelt).

```html

</tr>
```

Ok, so from scratch what I do to install is:

1) Install Minconda (if you don't already have it) from [here](http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html)
2) Install git (again, only if you don't already have it) from [here](https://git-scm.com/downloads)

Then once you have those in place:

Create a new conda environment to keep any existing Python code on your system away from your new project.
`conda create -n eppy-env python`

Activate the conda environment.
`activate eppy-env`

Clone (download) the latest version of eppy using Git.
`git clone https://github.com/santoshphilip/eppy.git`

Change into the `eppy` directory.
`cd eppy`

Checkout the branch you want.
`git checkout i62_eplusrunner`

Install eppy (this might be the step you're missing).
`pip install .`

You'll also need to install `tinynumpy` to run the tests.
`pip install tinynumpy`

Run the test suite (the `-v` flag gives verbose output from the tests)..
`py.test eppy -v`

If you still have errors, post the output of the tests here.

Thanks for making this demonstration 👍 

I totally see your point, it is valuable to avoid getting buried in maintaining backwards compatibility if it doesn't cost us much. 

Without having a dedicated `button_toggle` there is only a slight chance to hurt ourselves: by accidentally generating too many events with mouse scroll. But when this happens, the impact is low, both here and in `execute_script`. So I agree for not having a dedicated button to generate a fortune or show a notification with full script output.

`button_refresh` also makes sense to remove in this module. I wouldn't say `button_refresh` only relates to Internet, I think it can be generalized to "when it is expensive to refresh often". Internet traffic is valuable, so refreshing always is bad. External scripts can be slow or consume lots of CPU, so refreshing always is again bad. Generating fortunes is fast, so I don't see any issue with generating them on any click.

So, I think in the end we did agree on when to have a dedicated button and when not 🙂 

The only disagreement we have is what is better, have a button that by default does nothing, or let this button by default do some action (show notification) since it is unused — for that we are waiting third opinions 😉

```python
import string
import random
import asyncio

from prompt_toolkit import prompt_async


async def noise():
    s = string.punctuation
    while True:
        n = random.randint(10, 50)
        print(''.join(random.choice(s) for _ in range(n)))
        await asyncio.sleep(2 * random.random())


async def prompt():
    while True:
        result = await prompt_async('Say something: ', patch_stdout=True)
        print('You said: %s' % result)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.create_task(noise())
    loop.run_until_complete(prompt())
```

I think we should keep ``make_class`` but make it just a simple utility function that would delegate to ``dataclass`` and would look approximately like this:
```python
def make_dataclass(cls_name: str,
                   fields: Iterable[Union[Tuple[str, Type], Tuple[str, Type, Field]]],
                   bases=(), ns=None):
    anns = OrderedDict((name, tp) for name, tp, *_ in fields)
    ns = ns or {}
    ns['__annotations__'] = anns
    for item in fields:
        if len(item) == 3:
            name, tp, spec = item
            ns[name] = spec
    cls = type(cls_name, bases, ns)
    return dataclass(cls) 
```
I think programmatic creation of dataclasses will be useful, and providing a utility function that just takes care of ``__annotations__`` etc. will be helpful (it is however not necessary to devote any attention to it in the PEP). Example use:
```python
C = make_dataclass('C',
                   [('x', int),
                    ('y', int, field(default=5)),
                    ns={'add_one': lambda self: self.x + 1}])
```

Note that with the proposed signature we can remove ``name`` and ``type`` parameters to ``field`` as well, I am +1 on removing them.

The relation between formats of data types:
[user-input](https://github.com/mission-liao/pyswagger/blob/master/docs/md/ref/client.md) -> prim_factory -> primitives for pyswagger (abbreviated as pyprim) -> marshal -> json/xml

The correct procedure of unmarshal should be:
xml/json -> unmarshal -> user-input -> prim_factory -> pyprim

However, I didn’t follow this “expected” design and just make pyprim accept json as string-buffer.
ex.
pyswagger.primtives._model.Model.apply_with
pyswagger.primtives._model.Array.apply_with

Therefore, it works in pyswagger.io.SwaggerResponse.apply_with, because pyprim accept json string. But it’s not a correct design.

Here is the right design:
 - those ‘json.load’ in Array/Model should be removed
 - in pyswagger.io.SwaggerResponse.apply_with, when we encounter a ‘body’, this string should go through MIME codec, generate a correct input for prim_factory, and then send them to _prim_ if necessary.

it seems really messy in io-related code, and I can’t predict what would go wrong when reworking. So these are the follow-up options:
- option#1 is I merge your works to master, and I will follow your design to make things right.
- option#2 is let you finish these rework, and I will peer review your work.

What do you think? and thanks for your work again.

FYI: my threshold is on 0.5 as I found out that after normalisation, a lot of files (audio and video) are still at 0.3 or something. I can't hear the difference with 0.0 compared to other files.

Problem is, if you rerun, they'll keep being picked up for normalisation, being processed only to end up with a file that's 0.3 again.

You don't need a flag to mark a file as normalised. You need the level.

Even so, when you one day decide to set the level at -5db, those files wouldn't process anymore.

So for your problem, since it's already at 0.0, maybe set a threshold to 0.1 so that the file is skipped

I'm looking at your repository and I think I know why it is the way it is.  When you created the project, you probably did a 'git clone' of my repository, made your changes, then uploaded them into github.  What you should have done to make this easier is to create a "fork" of the tk-tools repository (top right within github) and then cloned from your own repository and made commits from there.  If you do forks in that manner, pull requests via github are basically one-button events.

# When not forked within github

There are probably a few ways to do this, and I'm not sure if this will work perfectly, but it is close.  What this procedure should do is create a fork of the original repository, download both of the repositories to your machine, do a merge on your local machine, then upload the changes back to the fork and do a normal pull request.

 1. Delete the repository on your local machine (it is on github, so no big deal) 
 2. On github, rename your 'naejgz/tk-tools' repository to something else like `naejgz/tk-tools2` to prevent naming conflicts
 3. Use the `Fork` button on the `slightlynybbled/tk-tools` to make a fork of my repository.
 4. Clone both repositories to your local machine.  This will take two `git clone http://...` commands.
 5. Navigate into the `tk-tools` and `git pull <branchname> ../tk-tools2/.git` (you may want to use absolute paths)
 6. Still within `tk-tools`, `git push origin <branchname>`
 7. Now use the 'Create pull request` button

# When forked within github

Within github, there is a button just above your files list which says "New Pull Request".  If you click that button, you will be presented with something that looks a bit like this issue, which is really a space to discuss the change.  It should show you if the current test suite passed and has some other information in different tabs for review purposes.

One of the boxes allows you to 'title' your change request and has another text box to describe the change.  Simply describe the intent and hit the "Create pull request" button.

We will likely enter into a brief discussion and I will merge the pull request when any breaking changes have been addressed (or accepted).

Thank you so much!

If you want, you can simplify your `for` loop like this:

```python
for face_location in face_locations:
    face_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(image, known_face_locations=[face_location])[0]

    if face_recognition.compare_faces(sample_face_encoding,face_encodings)[0]:
      print("Found!")
```

But as far as why it is returning True for each face, I'm not sure without seeing the images in question. You might try adding this line to your `for` loop to print out each face distance:

```python
    print(face_recognition.face_distance(sample_face_encoding,face_encodings)[0])
```
That will show you how similar each face is to your sample face in terms of distance. By default, `compare_faces` checks for faces less the 0.6 distance apart to count as a match. Maybe your faces are very similar and need a smaller distance. You can use a smaller distance tolerance like this with the tolerance parameter:

```python
    if face_recognition.compare_faces(sample_face_encoding, face_encodings, tolerance=0.5)[0]:
```

This is a problem with the Gaussian Process object. It usually happens when the function you are fitting the GP to is too jagged and the kernel you are using can't handle it. 

This is relatively common when running this method against scale parameters, where the difference between setting, say, C=e-6 and C=e-5, (could be) is just as meaningful as the difference between C=1 and C=10. If this is the case, you may benefit from maximizing against the logarithm of the parameter.

Hi @jaimegildesagredo,

I've worked on a couple of versions. The implementation is actually the same (and in fact very simple as you said), but the tests are different. In [this version](https://github.com/alejandrodob/expects/tree/not-equal-strict-comparison-support) I've added the tests in a different context, also making them very explicit. I do not like about them that they're a bit out of the line of the rest of the tests, as they are not really describing a spec, but an implementation detail maybe (by spying on the call to the `__eq__` method). On the other hand, [here](https://github.com/alejandrodob/expects/tree/not-equal-strict-comparison-support-v2) I've tried to keep the same existing tests, but instead of comparing numbers, compare instances of a class with a "special" `__eq__` behaviour so it fails for some special cases, thus making it easy to catch the bug for those special cases when using `not_to(equal)`.

I think that this second version, despite being a bit trickier, is more in the spirit of the rest of the project tests.

Please, take a look at both and tell me what you think, if you prefer any of them or they're both crazy :)
Thanks!

Here's some example config for you - this works with current stable version of Django storages:

| Name                          | Description                                                                  | Example value                                                      |
|-------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| AWS\_STORAGE\_BUCKET\_NAME    | The name of the storage bucket.                                              | mysite                                                            |
| AWS\_S3\_ENDPOINT\_URL        | The endpoint url excluding the bucket name.                                  | https://nyc3.digitaloceanspaces.com                                |
| AWS\_S3\_OBJECT\_PARAMETERS   | Default params for objects.                                                  | { 'CacheControl': 'max-age=86400' }                                |
| AWS\_LOCATION                 | The folder within the space to store files.                                  | mysite                                                            |
| AWS\_S3\_SIGNATURE\_VERSION   | Signature version. DigitalOcean only support v2 for pre-signed urls.         | s3=s3v2 (Version 2), s3v4=s3v4 (Version 4)                         |
| AWS\_STATIC\_LOCATION         | The folder within the space to store static files.                           | '%s/static' % AWS_LOCATION                                         |
| STATICFILES\_STORAGE          | The storage backend to use for static files.                                 | 'op\_storage.storage\_backends.StaticStorage'                      |
| STATIC\_URL                   | The base URL for generating URLs to static files included in rendered pages. | "https://%s/%s/" % (AWS\_S3\_ENDPOINT\_URL, AWS\_STATIC\_LOCATION) |
| AWS\_PUBLIC\_MEDIA\_LOCATION  | The folder within the space to store public media files.                     | '%s/media/public' % AWS_LOCATION                                   |
| DEFAULT\_FILE\_STORAGE        | The storage backend to use for public media files.                           | 'op\_storage.storage\_backends.PublicMediaStorage'                 |
| AWS\_PRIVATE\_MEDIA\_LOCATION | The folder within the space to store private media files.                    | '%s/media/private' % AWS_LOCATION                                  |
| PRIVATE\_FILE\_STORAGE        | The storage backend to use for private media files.                          | 'op\_storage.storage_backends.PrivateMediaStorage'                 |

### Private Configuration

| Name                     | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| AWS\_ACCESS\_KEY\_ID     | The access key for the storage API. Although this can be public, it's useful to store it with the secret key to force the server admin to think about / change it, at the same time as changing the secret. |
| AWS\_SECRET\_ACCESS\_KEY | The secret key for the storage API. Never reveal this to anyone :)                                                                                                                                          |

You can use the models/storage backends/views/etc from the blog post below to create a test site (called mysite) to try this out. Testing locally using runserver works.

https://simpleisbetterthancomplex.com/tutorial/2017/08/01/how-to-setup-amazon-s3-in-a-django-project.html

See the folder "s3-example-public-and-private" from the GitHub repo for that blog post: https://github.com/sibtc/simple-s3-setup

To get the access key and secret key, follow the getting started guide from DigitalOcean: https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/managing-access-to-digitalocean-spaces#option-1-%E2%80%94-sharing-access-to-spaces-with-access-keys

Although this solves a corner case with the way current django-tenant-schemas operates, after some additional investigation it is my belief that this should probably be happening on *every* set_schema() call in the tenant_schemas.postgresql_backend rather than just at the beginning of a request (in the middleware) or at the beginning of a task (in celery worker). It is not uncommon, from my experience, to change the schema while in middle of processing a request or in middle of a celery task. 

This also only is relevant if you use ContentType app in both SHARED_APPS and TENANT_APPS 
(which is the recommended setting by django-tenant-schemas). It could be set as an option to disable content type cache clearing as an optimization (default to on for safety, though).

In general, most mfcc programs wont give identical features due to offset
and/or scaling differences, as well as any of a number of other
implementation differences.  When building features though, these
differences won't matter, since the classifier will learn the supplied
features just fine. The real test is whether you get similar accuracies at
the output of the classifier, which should be the case.

If you are actually looking for binary compatibility with your other
feature generation script,  it will likely be a pretty difficult job.

Seems like this would be a great API compatible thing to implement. The `AsyncResult.get` API is available in all versions of Celery: [version 4](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/v4.0.2/reference/celery.result.html#celery.result.AsyncResult.get), [version 3](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/3.1/reference/celery.result.html#celery.result.AsyncResult.get), and the [archived version 2](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/2.5-archived/reference/celery.result.html#celery.result.AsyncResult.get).

There is some ongoing discussion and work you should check out in Issue #70 and PR #73 that are yet to receive comments from @codingjoe, the current maintainer, or the package author, but for me it seems that it would be great if we could either combine the Celery health checks to be compatible with all Celery versions (3 and 4 are already compatible, but I think we could support version 2 from the same code base just as well). I was personally dreaming about renaming the `health_check_celery3` backend to `health_check_celery` before cleaning the implementations up.

For case it seems like we could just use one health check module that would

1. Try to import Celery 4 or 3 and compatible task APIs, and check the Celery version. If both of those are unavailable:
2. Try to import Celery 2 and compatible task APIs, and check the Celery version. If that is unavailabe as well:
3. Raise an ImportError with stacktrace about all Celery packages being unimportable.

I also do not see any references to `url_prefix` in the [django-rest-framework code base](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/search?utf8=✓&q=url_prefix), so it's unclear to me in what way this PR uses the same naming.

As I mentioned [here](https://github.com/dessibelle/sorl-thumbnail-serializer-field/pull/3/files#r84162827) `HyperlinkedSorlImageField` attempts to handle `to_representation()` transparently, in the regard that it attempts to find a sorl generated thumbnail and builds the URL from that. In case it fails, it should fallback to the default `rest_framework.serializers.ImageField` behaviour. This PR appears to break that by requiring `request` if I interpret the code correctly.

In large part I would say the solution is rather a special Storage class as suggested in https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/pull/4577 that would handle e.g. the CDN use case. I may be wrong of course, in which case I would prefer a `url_prefix` kwarg which does not break the current behaviour of `HyperlinkedSorlImageField`, or perhaps even a specific subclass if such explicit dependence on `request` is in deed needed.

`tf.QueueBase.dequeue_many(n, name=None)` allows `n` to be a tensor. A new proposal is to have two different queue tokens - one for announcing the number of upcoming data ones. On the intake side of the queues the feeding thread would buffer samples till an aggregated sample length is reached and then send out an announcement token containing the number of samples followed by the actual sample tokens.
The aggregated sample length to required memory ratio can be determined one time and used as a factor for different GPU memory configurations.
Finally the `lerning-rate` parameter of `tf.train.AdamOptimizer` accepts a tensor and could therefore be used to dynamically adapt to the now changing (dynamic) batch sizes.
We can also build the node wrapper with `-Wl,-rpath,$ORIGIN/` and ship all `.so` next to `deepspeech.node` (or in a clever way, but the process remains close).

@krzysztofwolski @patrys So for last few hours I was looking for solution how to handle normalization when currency has 3 decimal digits - and I must say - there is no pretty solution for that.
Currency formats available through `babel` don't define 3 decimal digits. We can achieve that only by setting `currency_digits=True` in `format_currency`, but it will be always using currency's number of decimal digits (even if normalization is set to true)
Right now I have two options:
First - don't allow for price normalization when price has 3 decimal digits.
Second - make a little hack:
```python
currency_fractions = get_currency_fraction(currency)
if currency_fractions == 3:
    pattern = pattern.replace('.00', '.###')
else:
    pattern = pattern.replace('.00', '.##')
```
What do you think?

While looking at this, I remembered why it was written how it was - I wanted it to not fail when the encoding was weird, but that isn't the best behavior.

In this case, as PGPy isn't a character encoding detection library (and reliably detecting charsets is non-trivial), I think the "right" approach here is to do the following:

 - Expect that text data given to PGPy when creating objects is UTF-8 (or ASCII, which is compatible anyway) and raise an exception if that isn't the case

 - Respect the "Charset" armor header (see [RFC 4880 6.2 - Forming ASCII Armor](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4880#section-6.2)) when present. Internally, PGPy will still store text data as UTF-8 bytes, but should encode to the charset given if that header is present.

I don't think you are "breaking your schema" there are several different representations of an Integer such as a string or as a number. In JSONAPI spec the [id field is a string](http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-resource-object-identification), but in the database the column is an integer. So to me it seems reasonable to make the Schema handle the translation from database (integer) to JSONAPI (string that happens to be an integer).

I will also note that the examples in this project define the id field in the schema as a [string field](https://github.com/miLibris/flask-rest-jsonapi/blob/master/examples/api.py#L48):

> `id = fields.Str(dump_only=True)`

My suggestion is perhaps a better solution because you get the benefit of `fields.Integer` validation that `fields.Str` won't provide. Of course if you prefer your solution then I'll stop trying to convince you. I am just trying to find you a fix that works now. Best of luck!

Taking a look.  We have specific tests in place to ensure we don't leak memory when creating clients: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/tests/functional/leak/test_resource_leaks.py#L39-L65

One of the things I've noticed from your code is that you're assigning a `__del__` to a client, which in this scenario will prevent the object from being gc'd because of the cycles created within a client.  This isn't specific to botocore, this applies to any python class:  For example:

```
import gc


class ObjectWithDel(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def __del__(self):
        # This method will never be called because we'll create a cyclic reference.
        print "__del__ called for:", id(self)


class ObjectWithNoDel(object):
    pass


def test():
    a = ObjectWithDel()
    a.a = a

    b = ObjectWithNoDel()
    b.b = b
    print "created a, id(a) ->", id(a), "type:", type(a)
    print "created b, id(b) ->", id(b), "type:", type(b)


test()
gc.collect()
print gc.garbage


$ python test.py
created a, id(a) -> 4353206544 type: <class '__main__.ObjectWithDel'>
created b, id(b) -> 4353206608 type: <class '__main__.ObjectWithNoDel'>
[<__main__.ObjectWithDel object at 0x10378a910>]
```

However, the real botocore client's don't actually implement a `__del__` so this behavior isn't something you'll actually see in real code.

Right but I want the questions i asked answered. I am not super interested in talking about how pinning works because I know that already, I’m interested in UX. That’s why I was asking about the actual interaction. 

For context, I don’t really want a bunch of random garbage in pipfiles that we arbitrarily decide on. I am currently of the opinion, and have always been, that these _default behaviors_ should be just that — behaviors performed during lock. So if we want a loose pin on major version to be the default, why do we need to put that in every entry? We can just lock with that behavior and use the old lockfile as a reference. 

In the same vein, maybe the actual correct behavior of the resolver is something that isn’t expressed as a pin, but if I can see a use case I can understand how it might work. Because so far I see that we shouldn’t break things, but that’s super vague. Not everyone uses semver and it’s not necessarily sensible to force major version pins. I suspect that would cause a LOT of dependency conflicts unnecessarily. 

I get the impulse. But understand that a resolver only works when its resolution space is unconstrained enough to find matches. That’s the whole point of having one. If you are going to upgrade one thing it makes a lot more sense to let the resolver sort everything out than to hand back 20 resolution errors; it also asks a lot more of the user if you go that route. Most importantly If you as a user make a series of upgrades, now you have either to go one by one unpinning things and resolving again or you have to update each major version pin by hand. That doesn’t seem like a good UC when we have lockfiles for pinning


I have yet to encounter a situation where it would be easier to do these unpinning steps than to see that your app dependency went up a major version and add just a single loose pin. So at the end of the day I feel like sure, we can argue about whatever, but I care a lot about what we are asking users to do and how much work that will take and how hard it will be and how intuitive and how smooth or pleasing. And if I think something makes the experience worse, I care about the alternatives and how good they are, and the impact of the problem. 

If you know where else this has come up I’d love to get a list together to get a good understanding of shah it is that people need. 

One last point: I want to reiterate that we need to drop the assumption that all code is semver and all semver only breaks with major releases. It simply isn’t the reality and I don’t think it’s fair to make the assumption on users behalf that all of these things are true of their dependencies.

Ended up with overriding `Plugin.attach` and `ImageViewer.__add__`, so that `ImageViewer.original_image_updated` doesn't trigger all plugins and thereafter the linked plugins (which results in a waterfall of `filter_image()`).

**Plugin.attach**
```
class Plugin(base.Plugin):
    "override attach"
    
    def attach(self, image_viewer):
        """Attach the plugin to an ImageViewer.
        Note that the ImageViewer will automatically call this method when the
        plugin is added to the ImageViewer. For example::
            viewer += Plugin(...)
        Also note that `attach` automatically calls the filter function so that
        the image matches the filtered value specified by attached widgets.
        """
        self.setParent(image_viewer)
        self.setWindowFlags(QtCore.Qt.Dialog)

        self.image_viewer = image_viewer
        if len(image_viewer.plugins) == 0:
            self.arguments = [image_viewer.image]
            image_viewer.original_image_changed.connect(self._update_original_image)
        else:
            self.arguments = [image_viewer.plugins[-1].arguments[0]]
            image_viewer.plugins[-1].image_changed.connect(self._update_original_image)
        
        image_viewer.plugins.append(self)
        self.filter_image()
```

**ImageViewer.__add__**
```
class ImageViewer(viewers.ImageViewer):
    "override plugin add to not emit plugin.update"
    
    #copied from scikit-image
    def __add__(self, plugin):
        """Add plugin to ImageViewer"""
        plugin.attach(self)
    #    self.original_image_changed.connect(plugin._update_original_image)

        if plugin.dock:
            location = self.dock_areas[plugin.dock]
            dock_location = Qt.DockWidgetArea(location)
            dock = QtWidgets.QDockWidget()
            dock.setWidget(plugin)
            dock.setWindowTitle(plugin.name)
            self.addDockWidget(dock_location, dock)

            horiz = (self.dock_areas['left'], self.dock_areas['right'])
            dimension = 'width' if location in horiz else 'height'
            self._add_widget_size(plugin, dimension=dimension)

        return self
```

> Since this is now part of the CI service on GitHub and therefore more visible, signing should not impact it?

Yes, the automated kwalitee run via CI plays an important role.  We seem to drift a bit from the original "social" use case towards "single-author" use case, but as this is tightly related indeed, let me answer in quite some detail.

I agree fully with your statement especially for smaller projects such as `Flask-Menu`, where we rely on CI green/red light for everything, because we have extensive test cases covering most of the code base, and we run even `pep257` check as part of the Travis CI build.  When a build fails because `pep257` was not respected, the PR does not get merged.  We can say that the code kwalitee is fully exposed and checked as part of the "merge process".

However, for the Invenio package itself, we are not there yet.  We are only approaching this situation asymptotically.  Consider the following points: (in increasing order of difficulty)

- [easy] Kwalitee for `legacy` does richer testing than kwalitee for `master`.  (For example `run_sql()` checking, which may still be important for legacy modules in `master`; see past discussions [like this one](https://github.com/jirikuncar/kwalitee/issues/1)). Technologically, this is easy to solve, we just have to enrich `kwalitee` tool enough to cover everything we may want to cover.

- [easy plus social] Looking at past PRs, there were often cases where the CI builds indicates failure, yet the branch was merged.  This is due to kwalitee failing because it is checking the complete file PEP-8 compliance, while we have many holes there.  Technologically, we can solve this by amending kwalitee and paying attention only to differences that the given branch brings (as we do in `legacy` kwalitee), for example.  Sociologically, the kwalitee CI green/red light in PRs seems to be disregarded though because of this problem. It is mostly the test results that seem to be looked at in the CI. Socially, can we say that people are relying *today* on all kwalitee findings from CI and fixing them as part of the merge?

- [harder] Speaking of the importance of tests, in `legacy`, there are quite a lot of additional acceptance/functional/regression testing being done as part of CI due to the presence of the demo site.  In `master`, with Invenio as a "true" library framework, it does not make sense to run these, they were separated away into `invenio-demosite` package.  However, the moved-away tests are not fully represented yet in the Invenio core framework code base.  So when someone pushes a feature requests, we don't know whether it was tested in the core only, or in some overlay conditions only, or in more overlays, and whether the PR does not break our example vitrine, the Invenio demo site.  Technologically, this is harder to solve, because the best would be obviously to enrich Invenio core package test suite itself in order to cover *much more* use cases. (Plus we have additional demo site Jenkins nightly job for nightly testing.)  Practically, adding new tests to cover the full code base takes time; we are not there yet.  So, paradoxically, one could muse that `master` may be "less tested" than `legacy` today, due to split-away demo site.  Hence usefulness of possibly capturing QA signatures to express these "external conditions" while we are getting there.

- [only social] The current discussion seems to have turned more into what QA signatures the original author should use for himself, and that self-signing-for-kwalitee shouldn't be really necessary anymore.  While I agree that this is asymptotically solvable by richer kwalitee run with richer test suite and richer coverage, the CI process cannot express the notion that X tested the branch or Y reviewed it or that it runs on Z production system.  This is a matter of social conventions only, hence target of this RFC.

In summary, while we are probably in full agreement that richer kwalitee/tests/coverage in combination with CI findings can kill the necessity of signing one's own commits to indicate "self-kwalitee" -- and we are already there for Flask-Menu and friends -- what we perhaps disagree about is how far we are from this ideal state with Invenio package itself.  Asymptotically speaking, we are getting there, but we are not there yet.  However, even when we get there, we may need to express any notion of "social review" manually; hence the question of whether capturing the review process into some formal signed-off-by/tested-by/reviewed-by system is still useful of not.

>For the last one though, should we allow this without error, and give a flux of 0? Or do you think the error is the right runtime behavior?

We raise an exception when requesting the flux density of an SED outside of its defined range, so I think the right behavior is is to raise an exception for calculateFlux when that function would have asked about an out-of-bounds wavelength.  I agree that it would be helpful for that message to come from calculateFlux itself though.

(Note that the opposite behavior is true for Bandpass though, where requesting the throughput for an out-of-bounds wavelength does return 0.0.  My intuition for both of these choices was that users probably don't mean that an object's SED is really zero outside the range they specify, it's just unspecified.  But they probably do mean that a Bandpass throughput is zero outside of the specified range.)

The steps to resolve it are:
1. `git pull`  (You already did this, I think.)  It tells you there are conflicts.  I think probably just in Silicon.cpp.
2. Edit Silicon.cpp and look for where there are `<<<<` and `>>>>>` lines.
It will show both the version you had before the merge and what the changes were that it was trying to merge that it couldn't figure out how to resolve.  Decide which parts you want to keep in the merged version and delete the rest.  Usually this involves taking some bits from each part, but not necessarily.
3. Git `commit -a`.  This will finish the merge commit that it had started and paused.  So the commit message should already be correct for the merge.
4. Install and run the tests to make sure the merge didn't break anything.
5. `git push`.

And if you have trouble, feel free to ping me with questions, or even to push it broken, and I can fix it up.

> can we use gsparams to force a higher maxk and see if that fixes the issue? It would be nice if the fix was as easy as that... but I'm guessing you may have already tried this?

No.  The problem is slightly different than I described.  The problem is that for a convolution, we take the maxk to be the minimum of any of the component maxk values, the idea being that if it's effectively zero above that k for one of them, then it is also zero above that k for the product of all the items.

However, in the metacal case, above some k, the PSF is effectively zero.  But Deconvolve(psf) is effectively infinity.  So the product is near unity.  So the real maxk to use is from the rest of the items aside from these two.  Namely the "profile" of the noise correlation function, which is an autoconvolution of a  pixel.  That has a much higher maxk, so the value comes out badly wrong when you use the maxk of the PSF.

You are definitely right about pg_hba (and I envisioned it), because it must contain some custom network ranges. Everything else is pretty static and I kind of not implemented this functionality intentionally.

``postgresql.parameters`` -- usually this is something what you want to have similar across all cluster nodes. In order to solve this problem we introduced dynamic global configuration. Nodes are looking on ``config`` key and if there are some changes they are trying to apply them (including ``postgresql/parameters``).

I was thinking it would be not a problem to put some small static file into a docker:
```yaml
bootstrap:
  dcs:
    ttl: 20
    loop_wait: 10
    postgresql:
      parameters:
        shared_buffers: XXX
        max_connections: 200
        etc: ...

  initdb:
  - encoding: UTF8
  - data-checksums
  pg_hba:
  - line1
  - line2
  
```

Later it's possible to change global dynamic configuration  via API: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/docs/dynamic_configuration.rst#rest-api for all cluster nodes.

@hjacobs So I think we're sage from a security PoV to trust request.remote_addr. X_FORWARDED_FOR all depends on whether we are behind a trusted proxy or not. The only way I can see of indicating that the application is running behind a trusted proxy would be with an application configuration setting.

One option here, for a quick and clean commit, might be to log remote_addr and accept that this logging feature is not accurate behind a proxy (it will log the proxy). Obviously it's just a log line and no clients will get blocked unless a service such as Fail2ban is set up on the server. In some ways I'd prefer this option than add the risk of introducing spoofable IP addresses into the application.

@ssbarnea That is exactly the problem. Why is the library not separate from the executable? That executable (other package) could depend on jira library.

Alternatively, if you want to keep them together then `jira` should use `extras_require` and have people who want to use it as cli tool install it with `jira[cli]` where the `cli` extra would require `ipython`. That's similar to how one can install `ipython[notebook]` to get extra dependencies needed for IPython notebooks.

Most of the people don't need ipython in their apps. They would continue depending on `jira` and they would get only dependencies needed for the library. The ipython dependency would be excluded without an extra being required.

I can send a pull request for that change if you want although it would be nice if maintainers actually reverted the incompatible change made recently in #361.

The issue is not in whether ipython can be installed, but in the number of unnecessary other dependencies it can bring or just a simple fact that it's an unneeded dependency that is very large.

Don't forget that most large Python shops install packages using a build system of some sort, not by running `pip install` manually. This change would affect those users the most. Providing an `extras_require` correctly would prevent surprises in such builds.

Weiming,
The gridding uses a region of influence to determine what data points get
included into each CAPPI point. Picture it as a little sphere around each
data point on your new grid that determines what is close enough to be
combined.This is not being changed in yours and so the only thing that
differs is what Z level you're calculating it at.
For your first request if you want a CAPPI at say 100m, set min_z=max_z=100
and the number of Z levels to 1.

For the second thing you're trying to accomplish (Projecting the 3d data
onto the ground plane), I would grid multiple levels with min_z=0,
max_z=1000 for instance, and then grid multiple levels (say 5 levels for
every 200meters) which is the first argument to grid shape. That gives you
a rectangular grid. Then pick what aggregation operator you would
like(mean, sum, etc) and apply it in the vertical direction.
The alternative is to set the Z region of influence to be very very
large(In this case 1km), and grid at the ground plane.

> I'm trying to think of cases where the user might have more complicated authentication ...

I would just recommend making your proxy respect the `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` command, or otherwise have a way to specify authentication, e.g.:

```
$ your-proxy --use-gce-auth
$ your-proxy --use-service-account /path/to/service_account.json
$ your-proxy --use-bearer-token "bearer_token"
$ env GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=/path/to/service_account.json your-proxy
```

in general, a `.pyc`-only pex could present limitations in terms of python interpreter compatibility since `.pyc` files encode the compile-time magic number and check against their runtime interpreters magic number. this would prevent e.g. an otherwise python2+3 compatible pex from executing against both interpreters.

however, for controlled use in an environment where this isn't a concern, seems completely reasonable to have this capability behind a feature flag.

I gather node.js is a pretty fast moving target, so it seems likely the node.js travis VM image has custom installed newer versions of node and/or related software which we can't simply install onto a different image with apt-get.  We might be able to test with the packaged versions, but that isn't ideal.

To handle Python and C, maybe starting from the Python image and installing the relevant packages for C compilation would work, but as soon as we want the custom-installed versions from more than one VM image that trick stops working.

I'm not sure it's worth putting energy into working around these deficiencies in travis - hopefully they'll move on to a newer Linux version soon and this won't be such an issue.

I'll try to address your points one by one:

## Docker
I love docker.  We use it at work for precisely the same reason you mentioned, reproducibility.  When the user runs tests they should run the same way on their machine as they do on a build machine.  The reason why I don't believe we need docker here is:
- Docker helps in isolating binary dependencies for reproducibility.  I don't see any here (let me know if I am missing anything)
- For python dependencies, we are gaining an isolated python environment (and are using an explicit version of python) through the use of a virtualenv without the need of docker because it works on all platforms
- I would also argue that `pip install tox` is much less of an installation requirement than ensuring that docker works locally on your machine
- Yes docker layers are cached, but rebuilding a docker every time you want to do a build (some layers will break the cache) is not fun and working inside of a docker precludes you from using all of the tools that you normally develop with on your system. 
- Docker needs to be installed on the system

I think using docker boils down to the fact that you are trying to make the build systems/ testing the same across all of the crazyflie projects to make it easy for people to get started.  I am looking at this as an isolated project in which to develop for and I believe that is the best way to look at the situation.  If I was contributing to a ruby project I would expect to use those tools, java, those tools.  In fact as a contributor, I would probably have a language of choice and would be comfortable/willing to learn how to use the common tools for that language.  We also try to make all the steps very easy to follow, ie to get started testing this package:
``pip install tox``
``tox``
If I am a python developer, I would be very comfortable with this environment.  If I was new to python I would be encouraged that this is what the community is using.  I would also not be wedged into using 'specific' python technologies.

One of my initial complaints with using the crazyflie client was that it required an elaborate install process or a vm to get up and running.  It took me quite a bit of time to get up and running.  With this package all I need is python installed on my system and I can immediately start developing for the crazyflie.

## Toolchain
Can you describe more what you mean here about installing a full python environment?
We test in a virtualenv because it will not mutate the global python state, but by no means is it a restriction here. 

## Travis
I have used both Travis and CircleCI.  Both provide support for Docker and both provide python and tox support.  CI will support toolchains for whatever language you choose.  This is another reason that I vote for using the toolchain for the language.  We are, by no means, tying ourselves to travis with the travis file I have created.

## Answers to your questions
Do you think it would be possible (or worth) implementing a similar functionality in a docker container? 
Possible, but I still don't see the benefit that we gain here based on comments above.

My python knowledge is fairly limited, but are there any tools around that can run tests on different environments that we could add the to builder image?
Not quite sure what we are talking about here.  Are we talking about the Docker Image? What environments are we talking about, linux environments, language environments?  If we are talking about python versions, tox covers that.  If we are talking about linux versions, CircleCI does a good job at running your tests on different linux distributions in a parallelizeable manner.  If we are talking about osx, windows and linux, yes I think we can do that as well, but the code will be different per CI platform that we would want to go with.

## Final Message
I can help you do whatever you want to do for this project.  I just want you to understand that some of the choices that are being made may, in fact, increase the complexity of the project because of the global perspective that you are thinking about things in.

As @stuarteberg said, I thoroughly revised conda's version comparison just a few months ago. It is clean, carefully tested and well documented code that mostly conforms to PEP 440 and gives good error messages for syntactically incorrect version strings. The remaining differences to PEP 440 are deliberate:
* While PEP 440 rejects non-conforming version numbers, conda parses and compares them with essentially the same rules as the conforming ones. This is especially important for non-Python (e.g. C/C++ and R) packages that are outside of PEP 440's scope. I also needed this capability to work around the limitations of conda's feature resolution by means of version tags like `1.2.3.vc11` (rejected by PEP 440), but this may no longer be necessary after release of conda 4.
* PEP 440 allows version components to be separated by `'.'`, `'_'` and `'-' `. conda disallows the latter character because it already has a different meaning here.
* PEP 440 requires the letters `'c'` to be equivalent to `'rc'` and `'r'` to be equivalent to `'rev'` and `'post'`. conda treats `'c'` and `'r'`as normal characters for three reasons:
   * The PEP 440 convention is incompatible with existing conventions outside the Python world. For example, `openssl` counts versions with letters and requires `c < r < rc`. PEP 440 implies the order `c == rc < r` and disallows charcters `d, e, ...` entirely.
   * In practice, equating `'c'` with `'rc'` and `'r'` with `'rev'` is a non-issue as long as all packages adhere to *self-consistent* versioning. That is, packages should not switch arbitrarily between `'c'` and `'rc'` or `'r'` and `'rev'`, but stick to the convention originally chosen. Since version numbers are never compared across packages, different conventions will never occur in the same comparison expression.
   * The PEP 440 special cases may result in unpleasant surprises for users that don't read the smallprint to the end, i.e. for almost everyone.
* PEP ignores version component separators that are immediately followed by a letter, whereas conda always respects version components. Thus, PEP 440 interpretes `1.2.dev2` as `1.2dev2`, resulting in the order `1.2a1 > 1.2.dev2`. In contrast, conda interpretes `1.2.dev2` as `1.2.0dev2`, resulting in the order `1.2a1 < 1.2.dev2` (due to the rule that `1.2a*` precedes `1.2.0*`). I recall that the PEP 440 convention had certain undesirable side effects, but can't remember exactly which. In practice, this is again a non-problem when version numbers are self-consistent in the sense that letters are always added to the same version component.

A small omission of conda's code is that `rev` is not equivalent to `post`, but this is trivial to correct.

Regarding PEP 508: According to the documentation, conda does not currently support the operators `===` and `~=`. If desired, these operators should be easy to add without resorting to a major new library. I didn't yet check the other changes proposed in PEP 508.

Hi @Cherishty, recursively creating directories is not supported by the [REST API](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/create-directory) unfortunately. 

We did not add such overload in the SDK because each IO to the Storage Service incurs a cost, so the customer should consciously make each call.

The method is designed with the intention to be truly random, therefore it use `os.urandom` under the hood. If the goal is somehow to create a pseudo-random sequence then the mechanism should be implemented in another method.

Test and test framework must not rely on any repeatable random value between test runs. There is an existing mechanism in place to replace the random value with ordered, replayable moniker in recordings. Any attempt to replace it may compromise the parallel run because the duplicate names may prevent the live test from being successful.

I have a similar issue with one project. The main blocker is that aiobotocore has a very strict version dependency with botocore (currently 1.10.58), which makes it very hard to use with a different version of boto3 for example.

The idea to vendor botocore is to avoid this strict version dependency, so we can use them independently. The rest of dependencies (or transitive ones) don't need to be vendored since they don't pin to specific versions of these packages.

To summarize the 3 areas we need to solve that @rgbkrk listed:

1. The backend/filesystem layout of static assets.
2. How a frontend requests them (is it direct, is it a url path in the notebook server /kernelspec/ir/static/..., is it per running kernel).
3. How it ends up in the notebook document (per cell, metadata across the notebook).

On 1) my initial though is that because different deployment scenarios and frontend architetures will be so different, that we don't specify the filesytem layout. If we get into that, I can't imagine things get really difficult to reason about all of the different choices: inside/outside Docker, using conda or not, electron or server, where is the kernel running. By this, I mean that a given frontend should be able the use the information from parts 2/3 and translate that into whatever filesytem layout is needed. The other issue is how to deal with a node_modules that is effectively spread out all over the place between the main server and kernels. Do you end up with multiple deployment bundles? How do you deduplicate packages across them?

On 2) is it not sufficient to specify all the things using npm package names and versions? If not, what is missing? I am concerned about making decisions at this level that assume particular bundling tools or path conventions.

One 3) I do think it is pretty important that it is easy for track down all of the static assets for a single notebook, so those assets can be bundled in different contexts such as nbonvert/static, etc. That would seem to point to notebook level metadata, but it probably also has to be in the cells that use those assets? Maybe both? Not sure.

I guess that it happens because when you're using `srcdoc=`, the document inside the iframe doesn't have its own URL, so the browser uses the URL of the parent document.

If you have a Javascript handler for clicking a link, you often want to prevent the default action from running, so the browser doesn't go to the link URL. The correct way to do this is `event.preventDefault();`, though you'll see quite a bit of code using `return false;` for this purpose.

This should not affect existing code because it leaves requirejs intact.  Existing code only becomes unloadable via restful path when/if an npm module is used.  It is in that case that existing code must be loaded via a webpacked bundle.

There are millions of Jupyter Notebooks in the wild now. Because of that,
we are taking a moderately conservative stance on evolving the notebook
document format. For us to create a new cell type, we would need to be
convinced that most of our users need it and that we can support it in a
robust way across all the different contexts that notebooks are used in
(GitHub, nbviewer, live notebook, nbconvert, etc).

You are obviously free to create your own new notebook format using our
code (it is open source) and your own ideas+code.

However, we will likely be a bit protective about the branding and
trademark aspects of this. Using the name "Jupyter" to describe a JSON
notebook format spec or document that doesn't follow our official JSON spec
would confuse users. Thus, we would ask developers building such things to
avoid using the name "Jupyter" to describe those documents or spec. Things
like the filename extension should also be different so tools that work
with notebooks will not confuse the different formats.

Cheers,

Brian

I'm pretty much okay with this as default behavior. Despite my desire to do something about this for years - tools still don't have a way to specify tool files they require - so planemo just grabs pretty much everything that isn't a tool by default since these files don't hurt anything.

I consider the tool shed tarballs pretty much a build artifact and a black box - so there is no need in my mind to customize them extensively as long as they work. I understand that you and say @peterjc may care more about the tarball though - so it is possible to include and exclude files via directives in .shed.yml.

- https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_blast/blob/master/tools/blast2go/.shed.yml#L22

A future version of planemo and tools - may require that newer tools (using tool profile versions) require the tools annotate the files they need (this would be wonderful for pulsar) - and the default de-multiplexing behavior may change at that time (but probably only for these newer tools).

@hpk42 I actually started this but it turns out the way that tracing is accomplished kind of makes it a bit tricky with `_MultiCall` being statically assigned in the `PluginManager._inner_hookexec` `lambda`.

So I wanted to propose:

- each `_HookCaller` is instantiated with it's own `_MultiCall` and we revert to a `LegacyMultiCall` only when `'__multicall__'` is detected in a `hookimpl` signature (i.e. when `_HookCaller._add_hookimpl()` is called) 
- make tracing enabled per `_HookCaller` instead of globally
  - we can still keep the `PluginManager.enable_tracing()` method (which could now also accept an optional `hookname` arg) that does it globally but internally it will enable tracing on each `_HookCaller`. 
- this should let us get rid of the confusing indirection of `PluginManager._hookexec()` and `_inner_hookexec` because multicall objects will be instantiated once per `_HookCaller` instead of on every hook call.

Let me know what you think.

1. I agree that generating the full listing can be expensive.  The plan is to change the behavior so that it only reads the full listing on request, but that is a CWL spec change so it is going to take a little longer to roll out.  (In the interim we might be able to add a hint that changes the behavior for v1.0 documents.)

2. Cwltool should handle the case of inaccessible files more gracefully than crashing, but in general if you can't read all the files in a directory tree, the correct behavior is probably to fail.

3. The distinction between "location" and "path" is that "location" is a canonical URI representing a file or directory (which could be in remote storage) and "path" is where it is available on the local filesystem.  Files are only guaranteed to be staged when running a CommandLineTool.  ExpressionTools are executed as part of the workflow runner, which is not guaranteed to have local copies of the files being manipulated.

This complexity is present because CWL is intended to be able to describe distributed computations where the workflow runner, compute node, and storage may be three separate and distinct systems.

I think it wouldn't hurt to add it to the current RNN layers. We'd just have to decide about the interface:

1. A boolean flag telling to only keep the final output ([-1] of the scan, even if it was backwards), defaulting to `False`
2. An integer telling to only keep the final N outputs ([-N:] of the scan, even if it was backwards), defaulting to `'all'` or `None` or something like that
3. A slice telling which outputs to keep

The third option is a bit complicated to use, the most complex to implement (it would replicate some `SliceLayer.get_output_shape_for()` code), and difficult to document in combination with `backwards`.
The first option is the easiest to implement and to document, and would probably fit most use cases exactly (note that it would drop the time step dimension).
The second would be more flexible than the first, but it probably wouldn't drop the time step dimension for the common use case of setting it to `1`. Is there a use case for the second option?

> However, `~y_vr.nonzero()` does not seem to work.

Sorry, I thought it would give a boolean variable. Boolean variables have only been added recently, though, and boolean indexing is still under way (https://github.com/Theano/Theano/issues/5192). It could be `(1-y_vr).nonzero()` then, but that's probably not going to be any simpler or faster than your version.

> I agree with my implementation not working if there are no examples from both classes present in the batch. What do you think would be an appropriate return value in this case?

Probably zero, to just skip that batch without doing any harm (although this would still affect SGD+ methods like momentum or adam). Getting it to work nicely, without discarding batches, may need a clever batch iteration scheme or a pool of positive and negative examples kept around to augment batches as needed. But at that point it's not a drop-in replacement for existing loss functions.

> computing the loss on the entire training set every iteration

This circumvents many problems, but opens others.

Given that this is not an established loss and getting it to play nicely with stochastic updates might require some research, I guess we shouldn't include it into Lasagne for now and leave it up to users to copy/paste it if they want to play with it. If this loss can be shown to give an advantage over conventional losses (in a research paper or even just in blog posts or Kaggle competitions), we can include it with corresponding references. I highly encourage you to experiment with it and would be interested to hear back!

As for the Jupyter directories, nteract adheres to the [Jupyter file paths](http://jupyter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/projects/jupyter-directories.html) (or at least purports to). You can set environment variables to override `JUPYTER_CONFIG_DIR` for Jupyter and `IPYTHONDIR` for IPython. The second one is created by IPython, not by nteract. There is a difference when running nteract vs. the notebook in a conda environment, since it will use the sys prefix within the conda env.

## Overriding `IPYTHONDIR`

The kernel for the conda environment can set keys in `env`, including where you want the `IPYTHONDIR` to be. Example:

```js
{
 "argv": [
  "C:\/python\/python",
  "-m",
  "ipykernel_launcher",
  "-f",
  "{connection_file}"
 ],
 "env": {
  "IPYTHONDIR": "C:\/temp"
 },
 "display_name": "Python 3",
 "language": "python"
}
```

Admittedly I don't know where you'd pick the temp dir or how the paths would look inside the JSON without my Windows machine with me.

## Overriding `JUPYTER_CONFIG_DIR`

The only option I can think of here is to either:

1. modify the global environment variable (this seems rough considering the restrictions on the school systems)
2. create a shortcut to the app that sets the environment variable (at least I think this was available)

## Future

I'm open to ideas for how we could make this easier.

Okay. so the memory thing was a bug, and I fixed it. After that, you run into the problem that `getuid` is unimplemented. I fixed that. After that you run into the problem that sigprocmask is unimplemented. I didn't want to implement that in the main tree so I added a stub implementation to my test file. After that, you run into the problem that ioctl is unimplemented.

You see where this is going? The environment required by bash is really complicated. You'll need to keep going through this process, implementing any syscalls it needs (there are... a lot) one at a time.

I got pretty far for you, I implemented a bunch of stuff in angr proper (pull master) and wrote a few stub hacks for this project in particular. Here's what I have so far:

```python
import angr

# pylint: disable=arguments-differ,unused-argument

class rt_sigprocmask(angr.SimProcedure):
    def run(self):
        return 0

class rt_sigaction(angr.SimProcedure):
    def run(self):
        return 0

class ioctl(angr.SimProcedure):
    def run(self, fd, request):
        simfd = self.state.posix.get_fd(fd)
        if simfd is None:
            return -1
        else:
            return 0 # all ioctls on open files succeed

struct_sysinfo = angr.sim_type.parse_type("""
           struct sysinfo {
               long uptime;             /* Seconds since boot */
               unsigned long loads[3];  /* 1, 5, and 15 minute load averages */
               unsigned long totalram;  /* Total usable main memory size */
               unsigned long freeram;   /* Available memory size */
               unsigned long sharedram; /* Amount of shared memory */
               unsigned long bufferram; /* Memory used by buffers */
               unsigned long totalswap; /* Total swap space size */
               unsigned long freeswap;  /* Swap space still available */
               unsigned short procs;    /* Number of current processes */
               unsigned long totalhigh; /* Total high memory size */
               unsigned long freehigh;  /* Available high memory size */
               unsigned int mem_unit;   /* Memory unit size in bytes */
           }
""")

class sysinfo(angr.SimProcedure):
    def run(self, ptr):
        struct_sysinfo.with_arch(self.state.arch).store(self.state, ptr, {
            'uptime': 1234,
            'loads': [0, 0, 0],
            'totalram': 8,
            'freeram': 2,
            'sharedram': 2,
            'bufferram': 1,
            'totalswap': 8,
            'freeswap': 8,
            'procs': 10000,
            'totalhigh': 2,
            'freehigh': 1,
            'mem_unit': 1024**3,
        })

p = angr.Project('/bin/bash')
p.simos.syscall_library.add('rt_sigprocmask', rt_sigprocmask)
p.simos.syscall_library.add('rt_sigaction', rt_sigaction)
p.simos.syscall_library.add('ioctl', ioctl)
p.simos.syscall_library.add('sysinfo', sysinfo)

s = p.factory.full_init_state(mode='tracing', args=['bash', '-c', 'exit'])
simgr = p.factory.simulation_manager(s)

simgr.run(until=lambda lsimgr: len(lsimgr.active) != 1)
import IPython; IPython.embed()
```

However... the current thing it's failing on is `access(2)`, which requires a bunch of integration with the filesystem. You could see what happens if you implemented policies of "all files exist" or "no files exist", to see what happens, but I doubt that would work. You'll have to actually implement that syscall. The next-easiest way would be to have a specific list of arguments you have answers prepared for. The hard way would be to actually interface with our simulated filesystem. Luckily, a big refactor just landed making this not a disaster, but it'll still be some work.

One important thing you can do to make the API more predictable is wrap anything you want wrapped in a pointer with a `PointerWrapper`, a reference to which can be found in `project.factory.callable.PointerWrapper`, if I recall correctly.

Other than that, yes, the API is intensely ad-hoc and needs a principled redesign. I don't believe there is much in the way of dependencies on the existing behavior, and whatever you break probably won't have a hard time being rewritten if your redesign is as principled as promised.

The code below takes two input parameter and extract the depth stream, you can just add in the color stream and do it the way you do when you have a live realsense camera.

I think what went wrong is that you never start the pipeline using `pipeline.start(config)` 

```
import argparse
import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2
import os


CLIP_BG_FROM = 3.5
LOAD_BAG = 1
SHOW_COLOR = 0


def main():
    if not os.path.exists(args.directory):
        os.mkdir(args.directory)
    try:
        config = rs.config()
        if LOAD_BAG:
            rs.config.enable_device_from_file(config, args.input)
        pipeline = rs.pipeline()
        config.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 1280, 720, rs.format.z16, 30)
        pipeline.start(config)
        i = 0
        while True:
            print("Saving frame:", i)
            frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
            depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame()
            depth_image = np.asanyarray(depth_frame.get_data())
            cv2.imwrite(args.directory + "/" + str(i).zfill(6) + ".png", depth_image)
            i += 1
    finally:
        pass


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("-d", "--directory", type=str, help="Path to save the images")
    parser.add_argument("-i", "--input", type=str, help="Bag file to read")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    main()

```
