Hi @bocklund, many thanks for the links they look very useful! Do you have any plans to add any of that code to pymatgen? There's now a `pymatgen.io.atat` module, which is just very basic I/O currently. The plan was to add an SQSTransformation to `pymatgen.transformations.advanced_transformations` next, purely for provenance (so we can treat it like any other Transformation).

The intent with this atomate workflow was to make it easier to make our own SQS db, so hopefully we could on-demand generate SQS templates for some of the less common structures.

I'd be happy to incorporate any comments/suggestions you may have for this workflow. One issue is that we currently include the spectator atoms in the SQS generation (under the logic that these will change what clusters are generated, e.g. for ABO3 perovskites we include the O3), but it seems from your code that this might not be necessary, and just considering the disordered sublattice is sufficient? Are there any best practices you would suggest we follow? Happy to chat over email about this too.

Closing for now. Note that another option is that if a preset is "strong" enough (we really advocate for that preset, or there is a paper that advocates for that combination of settings that can be cited) we can create a new featurizer for it. That new featurizer can basically just call the old featurizer with the chosen parameter set. It should help in discoverability of that preset.

In your example, you don't really need to create AddressType, just only with `relay.ConnectionField(Address, edge_type=AddressTypeEdge)` will work.

Your example is failing because you are using LazyField in a resolved component (LazyField only wants either a string or a function for resolve once the schema is known).
So you can solve it doing:
```python
    edges = graphene.List(AddressTypeEdge)
```
Or
```python
    edges = AddressTypeEdge.List
```

This is a bug in the `GraphQL-core` (and `GraphQL-js` version up to 0.6).

Open question before merging: right now, gax does not directly depend on any of the transports needed for google-auth. It will check for requests, then httplib2, then give up [code](https://github.com/googleapis/gax-python/blob/master/google/gax/_grpc_google_auth.py#L38).

We have options:
1. We can leave this as-is and I think everything will be okay.
   * `google-cloud-python` will include httplib2 and `google-auth-httplib2` for now. `google-cloud-python` also never hits this code path.
   * We can make the `gapic-` packages depend on `httplib2` and `google-auth-httplib2` or `requests`, that ensures they always have a transport.
   * Alternatively we can make all `gapic-` packages upgrade to JWT credentials which do not need a transport. We should do this at some point anyway.
2. We can add a dependency here on one of the two transports.

@jagerman, I like that idea much better. I'll try to look at it in a bit.

> Ok, if that's the case, then this would be a great thing to call out in the documentation in the inheritance section.

This isn't really specific to pybind11. It applies to anything in C++: If you have an owned base pointer to a derived class, the base must have a virtual destructor. Otherwise, the behavior is undefined in C++. Pybind11 docs are pretty extensive as it is (it takes quite a while to read everything), so I'm not sure including C++ 101 topics is entirely useful.

One thing I don't fully understand is why `type_caster_generic` is not templated on the type. There's only 6 occurrences where it's used in the code, in all of which the type is known exactly. If it was templated, you could precache all kinds of things, e.g. registered_cpp_type for casts, direct converters for loads, etc.

Removing the order requirement in the ``class_`` arguments is nifty, and it nicely corresponds to similar behavior in parameter order independence in ``class_()`` and ``def()``. 

That said, I'm on the fence for this one for a couple of reasons:

With this patch, there would now be two 100% equivalent ways of specifying the base class (either as a variadic template argument, or via ``py::base<>``). As was discussed on the tracker a few times, a longer term plan of mine is to supplement ``py::base<>`` with ``py::bases<>`` for multiple inheritance (which is only instantiated when truly needed, hence ensuring compact code generation for the simpler single inheritance case). It was not clear to me when reading your patch that these things would be straightforward to do.

The amount of metatemplate programming that is necessary to pull this off makes for a gratuitously implementation. I expect that this also has an adverse influence on compilation time for big projects, where ``class_`` and members of ``class_`` are instantiated many times). Even if that is not the case, I am hesitant to introduce this much complexity.

> This (https://github.com/dean0x7d/overload_cast) is really neat by the way. Do you see any way to have a uniform syntax between the C++11 and C++14 versions?

I was hoping to, but I think the `overload<int>{}(foo)` version is the best that can be done in C++11 (macros don't save it either). I had an alternative idea which would need to be partially integrated with `def`, but I'm not sure how intrusive it would be. I'll get back to you with a concrete proposal.

> Yes, please add something to the docs (perhaps the first time arg is used in the basics part of the documentation? I would prefer it if you leave the first py::arg occurrence but then point out that there is also a shorter notation using user defined literals, as opposed to killing py::arg everywhere.)

Yeah, I was also thinking right around [here](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/basics.html#keyword-arguments) would be a good place to mention it, but only as the alternative syntax. But I'm not sure where to mention `str` and `_s`. Any suggestions?

> (note I'm using a special ACME server, not letsencrypt)

Which ACME server are you using? It's up to the CA policy whether to include one of the SANs in the Subject CommonName field. It's common practice to do so (in part because of breakage on macOS), but hostnames in CommonName are actually deprecated, and Let's Encrypt will eventually start issuing certain certificates without CommonName.

You may be able to solve your problem short term by providing a CSR to Certbot with `--csr`. In the long term, you should convince your CA to put something in the Subject when it would otherwise be empty. For instance, there's a proposal that's been put before the CA/Browser Forum to use dnQualifier for that purpose, which should work.

```
with requests.Session() as session:
    sf = simple_salesforce.Salesforce(..., session=session)
    sf.something()
```

Note that because the way that simple_salesforce currently does auth, it'll cause an authentication request to be sent every time you instantiate the class, so you don't want to do this all over the place.

Man pages are "unusual" for Python projects distributed via PyPI, and problematic, since they need to be installed into system directories. Sometimes, distro packagers add man pages when packaging Python stuff into deb/rpm etc., and that is "ok" (but not our task). 

I have strong opinions rgd distro packaging of Python stuff: it is plain wrong. Installing everything into the system/global Python is wrong - conflicting versions, interference between packages etc

That being said, I agree with @Eitot that `--help` should spell out all command line options, precisely and exhaustively. It sucks a little having to dig into source to get at the real stuff.

As a user, this would be my expectation of docs in general:

* there should be a concise README in the repo that is duplicated to appear as the PyPI page. Contents: what is it? why/for what use? what license / where did it came from (history). pointers to: source code, changelog, issue tracker, how to get in touch (contacts), docs (readthedocs)
* `--help`: concise, exhaustive command line usage/options
* readthedocs: tutorial like .. use case oriented. should we officially support API based usage, that too (API reference)

Sphinx is great .. the way to go IMO. Hosting: RTD generated from Sphinx. This can be fully automated (we can have RTD integrated, and online docs will be automatically updated). RTD is the go to place looking for Python docs.

---

Rgd CLI of Scour: currently we use `optparse` (Py stdlib). This is old, `argparse` (Py stdlib) is better and the new way. 

> Deprecated since version 2.7: The optparse module is deprecated and will not be developed further; development will continue with the argparse module.

Apart from argparse, we might also consider http://click.pocoo.org/. This can do very nice CLIs - though it would introduce a non-stdlib dep.

# Random technical notes

I was definitely going to ask about [bpo-32526](https://bugs.python.org/issue32526); that's a pretty obscure issue, so nice catch :-).

It's also legal to create an async generator in one task and then iterate it in another task. Actually I've even used this in an example... in the [live-coded happy eyeballs](https://github.com/python-trio/trio-talks/tree/master/njsmith-async-concurrency-for-mere-mortals), I cheat a little bit, because I claim that it's doing the same thing as the twisted happy eyeballs code, but twisted's version actually supports incremental `getaddrinfo`. (Which isn't supported by any real OS, but never mind.) But it's easy to adapt the example to handle the full twisted semantics: suppose we have an incremental `getaddrinfo` that returns an async iterator:

```python
async def fake_incremental_getaddrinfo(*args, **kwargs):
    for target in await getaddrinfo(*args, **kwargs):
        yield target
```

Then in the happy eyeballs code, each *child task* instead of doing `target = targets[i]` instead does:

```python
try:
    target = await targets_aiter.__anext__()
except StopAsyncIteration:
    return
```

You also need to do a bit of fiddling with the `failed_attempts` variable because you don't know ahead of time how many attempts there will be, but never mind -- the point is that if anyone ever does ask about the twisted semantics, then it's also quite easy to do and only adds a few lines. But... it's also a realistic case where iterating a generator in different tasks would be useful.

I suppose one could keep a global list of all living async generators, and scan them whenever a cancel scope is created or a task traps, but this would be way too expensive. (It might even be too expensive if we could restrict to a single task's generators... these are pretty performance-sensitive operations.)

Another potential way to map cancel scopes to async generators would be to use the `firstiter` hook to keep a global set of living async generator frame objects (!), and then when a cancel scope is created, check whether any of them are on the stack. On CPython at least this would be pretty quick (because now it's O(stack depth), not O(total number of generators in the program)). On PyPy it *might* be OK, I'm not sure – forcing the JIT to materialize frame objects isn't great, but if we're not looking at the contents of the objects, and it's only for async function frames in practice, then maybe it's not too bad?

# But let's step back a bit

The bigger question is what semantics we're even going for here. I think in this issue we should try to focus on just the GC issues, since they're simpler. (I added a link back to here from #264 though so the comments don't get forgotten.)

If you want to work on this, then probably the first thing that would be useful would be to figure out exactly what Python is doing right now, with no hooks defined :-). I wrote down a guess in the first post, but I haven't checked the details (which may require reading CPython's source and stuff).

There's also a simple thing we could do that might be better than what we do now, not sure: guarantee that all async generators will get `await trio.aclose_forcefully(agen)` called on them.
We now provide the cert chain as a list of blobs, so this would be even more straightforward.

Hi, @jordanorelli! As `plan_code` isn't a documented member of Subscription resources, I intended that you would access it through the subscription's `plan` object as you indicate. `plan_code` is in the `attributes` list so that it will be submitted to the service when creating or updating a subscription. Submitting a subscription sends only the plan code, not a whole complex `plan` element like Recurly returns in the response, so there should in fact be a `Subscription.plan_code` in that case.

However we did accept @perryism's pull request, so a `Subscription.plan_code` attribute (in the Python sense) is available in the master branch and will be in the 2.0.3 release of the library. HTH!
